# UniShelf

# **UniShelf**

## **Features:**

1. Multi format resource sharing (like videos, video links, pdfs, codes, images, etc.,.) \[download to device or view the resource anytime\]

2. Code sharing support \[full code or code snippets, GitHub repo links, syntax highlighted code viewer, copy code functionality\]

3. University specific ecosystem (Only users with university email ids can register, content visible only within the same university, acts as a single academic hub for the university)  
   Filter using tags through departments/course names.

4. Privacy & Sharing controls (Public Resources, Private Resources, Selective Sharing \[share resources with specific users , groups, optional time limited access\]

5. Search & Filters ( search by: title, description, tags, code language, filters like resource type , subject, semester, popularity, date, etc.,.)

6. academic organization ( course-wise resources, sem-wise grouping, subject-specific categories, exam-oriented collections)

7. Option to upload anonymously but the moderator/admin can see which account uploaded what (to verify reporting)

8. Each asset or user can have tags

9. Users can have atomic blacklist/whitelist for who can access the resource  
   

**USP:**  
University specific.

Why would g-drive not be a good fit ?  
It is not centralized, removes the need for multiple links, being able to search for things that were not shared to you personally.

**MISCELLANEOUS:**

File upload size (per upload) (depending on file extension)  
How would the tags work  
Custom university branding.  
Non-curated personalization and bookmarks.  
Page continuum within PDFs  
Homepage can be search box  
A tab including all documents with tags in common with the user  
Bookmarks tab  
“My uploads”/”My files” tab (like GDrive)  
Notes not accessed in (1 year?) can be moved to cold storage/de-listed from search  
Option to “surrender/transfer ownership” to make it publicly owned or change owner  
Permissions to be enforced in the backend or bucket level?  
Creator level access control (files inherit whitelists and blacklists)  
Handling external links  
Unique identifier for each asset

**Things not to include:**

- Automated content safety/moderation  
- User Engagement \[comments & discussion threads, likes and upvotes\]   
- group & community features \[groups and spaces like batch wise spaces, club & society spaces and project-specific groups, Seniors share notes & prep material, etc.,.\]   
- Code-centric features

## Commercial Aspect:

- Shared pool for whole university/user based storage limits  
- P2P?  
- Custom branding (they pay for logo, etc.)

[**Why UniShelf ?**](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1jFtAAQr__VLdDMR-62KXZj4tIAgabJ78tvZUzB9FdSk/edit?usp=sharing)

# Phase 1 template

---

**Study Project – Phase 1 Document Template**

*(Problem Identification & Planning)*

---

**Cover Page**

**Course Title:**

**Project Title:**

**Student Name(s):**

**Student ID(s):**

**Project Advisor / Supervisor:**

**Date of Submission:**

---

 

**1\. Project Idea Summary**

**Title of Project:**

**Abstract:**

Provide a concise overview of the project covering the following points:

* The problem domain  
* The motivation behind the project  
* The high-level solution approach  
* Expected impact or application

*(Recommended length: 150–200 words)*

---

 

**2\. Project Background and Motivation**

**Problem Statement**

Clearly define:

* The core problem being addressed  
* Who is affected by the problem  
* Why existing solutions are insufficient

**Motivation**

Explain:

* Why this problem is worth solving  
* Academic, technical, or societal relevance  
* Personal or course-driven motivation

---

 

**3\. Educational Value and Course Alignment**

**Relevance to Course Objectives**

Describe how the project aligns with:

* Course topics  
* Skills and concepts taught  
* Practical application of theory

**Learning Outcomes**

List the expected learning outcomes, such as:

* Technical skills  
* Analytical or design skills  
* Tool/framework exposure  
* Problem-solving and documentation skills

---

 

**4\. Objectives**

**Primary Objectives**

*    
*    
*  

**Secondary Objectives (Optional)**

*    
*  

---

 

**5\. Research and Analysis**

**Existing Solutions**

Briefly discuss:

* Existing tools, systems, or approaches  
* Their limitations or gaps

**Functional Requirements**

List key functionalities the system/project should support:

*    
*    
*  

**Non-Functional Requirements**

Include aspects such as:

* Usability  
* Performance  
* Security  
* Scalability  
* Maintainability

**Feasibility Analysis**

Comment on:

* Technical feasibility  
* Time feasibility (within semester timeline)  
* Resource constraints

---

 

**6\. Project Scope and Expected Deliverables**

**Scope Definition**

Clearly mention:

* What is included  
* What is excluded  
* Assumptions and constraints

**Deliverables (Phase 1\)**

Examples include:

* Project proposal document  
* Problem definition and objectives  
* Literature review / background study  
* High-level system overview

---

 

**7\. Preliminary Project Timeline and Milestones**

Provide a high-level timeline covering:

* Phase-1 activities  
* Tentative milestones aligned with the semester plan

*(Detailed Gantt chart is not required at Phase 1\)*

---

 

**8\. Team Structure and Collaboration**

**Roles and Responsibilities**

* Individual roles (or team roles for group projects)  
* Collaboration tools (e.g., GitHub, shared documents, task trackers)

---

 

**9\. Risk and Challenge Analysis**

**Identified Risks**

List possible risks such as:

* Technical complexity  
* Data/API availability  
* Time constraints

**Mitigation Strategies**

Explain how each identified risk will be handled or minimized.

---

 

**10\. Supervisor Review and Approval**

**Advisor Feedback:**

**Supervisor Comments:**

**Recommendations:**

**Signature:** \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**Date:** \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

---

 

# Phase 2 template

**Study Project – Phase 2 Document Template**

*(Design & Proof of Concept)*

---

**Cover Page**

**Course Title:**

**Project Title:**

**Student Name(s):**

**Student ID(s):**

**Project Advisor / Supervisor:**

**Date of Submission:**

---

 

**1\. Introduction**

**1.1 Purpose of Phase 2**

Briefly describe the objective of Phase 2, focusing on:

* Translating the Phase-1 problem definition into a concrete system design  
* Defining system requirements and architecture  
* Demonstrating feasibility through a Proof of Concept (PoC)

---

**1.2 Scope of Phase 2**

Clearly mention what Phase 2 includes, such as:

* System design and architecture  
* Detailed functional and non-functional requirements  
* High-level database and data flow design  
* A working or demonstrable Proof of Concept

---

 

 

**2\. System Overview**

**2.1 Product Perspective**

Describe:

* Whether the system is standalone or part of a larger ecosystem  
* High-level interaction between users, system components, and external services  
* Deployment environment (web, mobile, desktop, etc.)

---

**2.2 Major System Functions**

List the key functionalities supported by the system.  
Example:

* User authentication and authorization  
* Core application features  
* Data processing and analytics  
* External API or service integration

---

**2.3 User Classes and Characteristics**

Identify different user roles and briefly describe them.  
Example:

* End Users  
* Administrators  
* External Systems / APIs

---

 

 

**3\. Functional Requirements**

Clearly define what the system **must do**.

You may present requirements in one of the following formats:

* Numbered requirement list  
* Use-case based description  
* User stories

Example format:

* FR1: The system shall allow users to register and authenticate securely.  
* FR2: The system shall provide access to core features based on user roles.

---

 

 

**4\. Non-Functional Requirements**

Specify system quality attributes, including but not limited to:

**4.1 Performance Requirements**

* Response time expectations  
* Load handling assumptions

**4.2 Security Requirements**

* Authentication and authorization mechanisms  
* Data privacy and protection considerations

**4.3 Usability Requirements**

* Ease of navigation  
* Accessibility considerations

**4.4 Scalability and Maintainability**

* Modular design expectations  
* Ease of future feature extension

---

 

 

**5\. System Architecture and Design**

**5.1 System Architecture Diagram**

Include a high-level architecture diagram showing:

* Client-side components  
* Backend services  
* Databases  
* External services / APIs

*(Insert diagram here with a brief explanation)*

---

**5.2 Module-wise Design**

Describe each major module/component:

* Responsibilities  
* Inputs and outputs  
* Interactions with other modules

---

**5.3 Data Flow Design**

Explain how data flows through the system:

* Input sources  
* Processing stages  
* Storage and retrieval

*(Optional: Data Flow Diagram)*

---

**5.4 Database Design (If Applicable)**

Include:

* High-level schema / ER diagram  
* Key entities and relationships  
* Important attributes

---

 

 

**6\. Technology Stack and Justification**

List the technologies used and justify their selection.

Example structure:

**Frontend:**

* Technology name – reason for choice

**Backend:**

* Technology name – reason for choice

**Database:**

* Technology name – reason for choice

**Other Tools / Frameworks:**

* APIs, libraries, deployment platforms

---

 

 

**7\. Proof of Concept (PoC)**

**7.1 PoC Description**

Describe:

* What part of the system has been implemented as PoC  
* Purpose of the PoC  
* How it validates feasibility

---

**7.2 PoC Demonstration Details**

Mention:

* Screenshots, demo video, or live demo (if applicable)  
* Features demonstrated  
* Current limitations of the PoC

---

 

 

**8\. Testing and Validation Strategy**

Describe how correctness and reliability are validated:

* Unit testing approach  
* Integration testing approach  
* Manual or automated testing

---

 

 

**9\. Risks, Challenges, and Mitigation**

**Identified Risks**

* Technical complexity  
* Dependency on third-party tools or APIs  
* Time or resource constraints

**Mitigation Strategies**

Explain how each risk will be reduced or managed.

---

 

 

**10\. Phase 2 Outcomes and Readiness for Phase 3**

Summarize:

* What has been completed in Phase 2  
* System readiness for implementation  
* Key inputs carried forward to Phase 3

---

 

 

 

**11\. Supervisor Review and Approval**

**Advisor Feedback:**

**Supervisor Comments:**

**Recommendations:**

**Signature:** \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**Date:** \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

 

# Phase 3 template

**Study Project – Phase 3 Document Template**

*(Implementation Readiness & Validation)*

---

**Cover Page**

**Course Title:**

**Project Title:**

**Student Name(s):**

**Student ID(s):**

**Project Advisor / Supervisor:**

**Date of Submission:**

---

 

**1\. Introduction**

**1.1 Purpose of Phase 3**

Briefly describe the objective of Phase 3, focusing on:

* Demonstrating implementation readiness of the proposed system  
* Validating design choices through partial or complete implementation  
* Assessing system reliability, limitations, and future potential

---

**1.2 Summary of Work Completed So Far**

Provide a short recap of:

* Key outcomes from Phase 1 (problem definition & planning)  
* Key outcomes from Phase 2 (design & proof of concept)

---

**2\. Implementation Overview**

**2.1 Implementation Status**

Clearly state the current level of implementation:

* Fully implemented modules  
* Partially implemented modules  
* Planned but not implemented components

---

**2.2 Implemented Features**

List the features that have been implemented.  
Example:

* Core application functionality  
* Data handling and persistence  
* User interaction flows  
* Integration with external services (if applicable)

---

**3\. System Validation and Testing**

**3.1 Testing Strategy**

Describe the overall testing approach, such as:

* Unit testing  
* Integration testing  
* Manual or exploratory testing

---

**3.2 Test Cases and Results**

Summarize key test cases executed and their outcomes.

Example format:

* Feature tested  
* Expected behavior  
* Observed result  
* Status (Pass / Partial / Fail)

---

**3.3 Validation Summary**

Briefly explain:

* How the system meets the defined requirements  
* Any deviations from expected behavior

---

**4\. Performance and Reliability Analysis**

Discuss system behavior with respect to:

* Responsiveness  
* Stability  
* Resource usage (where applicable)

Mention any performance bottlenecks or constraints observed.

---

**5\. Risk Analysis and Mitigation Review**

**5.1 Identified Risks (Revisited)**

Revisit key risks identified earlier, such as:

* Technical complexity  
* Dependency on tools or APIs  
* Time or scalability concerns

---

**5.2 Mitigation Effectiveness**

Explain:

* Which risks were successfully mitigated  
* Which risks remain and why

---

**6\. Limitations and Constraints**

Clearly outline:

* Current limitations of the system  
* Assumptions made during development  
* Constraints due to time, resources, or technology

*(Clarity here is viewed positively in evaluations)*

---

**7\. Future Enhancements and Scope Extension**

List realistic future improvements, such as:

* Feature enhancements  
* Performance optimizations  
* Scalability improvements  
* Additional use cases or integrations

---

**8\. Learning Outcomes and Reflections**

Reflect on:

* Technical skills gained  
* Design and problem-solving insights  
* Challenges faced and lessons learned

*(This section is subtle but highly valued by faculty)*

---

**9\. Final Deliverables**

List all deliverables submitted as part of Phase 3, such as:

* Source code / repository  
* Executable or deployed system (if applicable)  
* Documentation  
* Presentation or demo material

---

**10\. Conclusion**

Provide a concise conclusion summarizing:

* Project readiness  
* Overall achievement of objectives  
* Preparedness for evaluation or deployment

---

 

**11\. Supervisor Review and Approval**

**Advisor Feedback:**

**Supervisor Comments:**

**Recommendations:**

**Signature:** \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**Date:** \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

 

# Unishelf features draft

| Primary Features (Must-Have for MVP) |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| These are essential for the core functionality and must be completed by Phase 3\. |  |  |
| \# | Feature | Description |
| P1 | University Email Authentication | OAuth login with email domain validation (e.g., @online.bits-pilani.ac.in) |
| P2 | User Profile & Role Management | Basic profile with year, department, enrolled courses; Admin/Student roles |
| P3 | File Upload System | Multi-format upload (PDF, images, videos, code files) with size limits per type |
| P4 | Resource Metadata | Title, description, course, semester, tags, visibility settings per upload |
| P5 | Anonymous Upload Option | Upload without displaying identity (admin can still view uploader) |
| P6 | Basic Search | Search by title, description, tags with text matching |
| P7 | Filter System | Filter by resource type, course, semester, department |
| P8 | Year-Based Access Control | Restrict content visibility based on student's academic year |
| P9 | File Download & View | Download files or view in-browser (basic preview) |
| P10 | Admin Moderation Panel | Approve/reject uploads, view uploader identity, manage reports |
| P11 | Resource Visibility Settings | Public, Private, or Course-restricted options |

| Secondary Features (Should-Have) |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Enhance user experience; implement if time permits after primary features. |  |  |
| \# | Feature | Description |
| S1 | Advanced Search | Full-text search with relevance ranking using Meilisearch |
| S2 | Blacklist/Whitelist per Resource | Uploader can block/allow specific users for their resources |
| S3 | Bookmarks/Favorites | Users can save resources for quick access |
| S4 | Quick PDF Preview | In-browser PDF viewing without download |
| S5 | Course-wise Organization | Browse resources grouped by course hierarchy |
| S6 | Semester-wise Grouping | Filter/browse by semester structure |
| S7 | Resource Reporting | Users can report inappropriate content |
| S8 | Upload History | Users can view/manage their uploaded resources |
| S9 | Code Snippet Viewer | Syntax-highlighted code display with copy button |
| S10 | Offline Access (PWA) | Save resources for offline viewing |
|  |  |  |

| Recommended Tech Stack: |  |  |
| ----- | :---- | :---- |
|  |  |  |
| **Layer** | **Technology** | **Reason** |
| Frontend | Next.js | SSR, API routes, fast development |
| UI Components | shadcn/ui \+ Tailwind CSS | Pre-built, customizable, free |
| Backend | FastAPI | Fast iteration, large ecosystem |
| Auth | (built into FastAPI) | Built-in OAuth, session management |
| Database | PostgreSQL | Relational data, JSONB for flexibility |
| ORM | SQLAlchemy | Type-safe, migrations, great DX |
| Object Storage | MinIO | S3-compatible, self-hostable, free |
| Deployment | Vercel \+ SupaBase  | Free tier available, easy deployment |
| —------------- |  |  |
| Caching | Redis | Session store, rate limiting |
| Search | Meilisearch | Easy setup, fast, open source |
| File Processing | Sharp (images), pdf-parse | Open source, efficient |
